author: @doriantinculas9396
text:
Hello !
What do you think of the Wells turbine?
In principle such bidirectional oscillatory motion achieved by your air motor is similar to the motion induced by the "oscillating water column" used to convert wave energy into electrical energy.

author: @dennisjohnson8753
text: Very nice

author: @RoofAndAMeal4UsAll
text: Wonderful!  How might those bellows hold up to heat?  A high temperature bellows that would not wear quickly would be awesome.  Thank you for your work Mr. Murray-Smith!

author: @quietparadise
text: hopefully i'm far behind or out of touch (quite common in my universe) but you have hopefully seen the wind turbine driven pumps that are simply prop, shaft, eccentric between bellows with appropriate check valves. simple concept. the ones i saw were using airbags meant for suspension for a semi truck ("lorry"?)

author: @themeek351
text: Well, I know what to make if I need an iron lung machine, lol!

author: @ktm42080
text: Great video! Thanks for inspiring us to think and experiment. I had teachers, back in the olden days, that would do similar things to motivate us to use our minds and turn off the TV.

author: @bettyswallocks6411
text: Add a set of tuned reeds and a keyboard producing a diatonic major scale to one set of bellows, and all you need is a bottle of rum and parrot. 😈🪗

author: @supermarketsweeps
text: if it is a heath robinson then you have to name it  puffgine

author: @bearnaff9387
text: What really makes these videos into treasures isn't the engineering-for-laypeople educational content, and it isn't the inventive shed tinkering.  It's the fact that your reaction to things working is a friendly and almost grandfatherly version of the Mad Scientist (Engineer, really) Cackle of Triumph.  You should be a fixture of US public television educational content.  Thank god Youtube exists to pick up the slack.

author: @maxv-o6u
text: Thanks for your continued inspirational videos❤

author: @CitizenSmith50
text: 2 bellows = overthinking it !    Use one with a return spring and simpler valve ?

author: @pgprentice
text: If I remember correctly they had something like this in the movie "The Great Escape",  to keep fresh air in the tunnel?

author: @MountainCatBob
text: Energy transmission by air pressure could be nearly 100% efficient where copper loses significant power in low voltage DC. Bellows are more fun than I ever imagined! Thanks -- Will definitely check out the Bellows series.

author: @bringtheideas460
text: Hey Robert. What do you mean the valve can be used for stirling engines? arent they valve-less?

author: @chriskeeble

author: @lawrencegt2229
text: Have you been watching the Great Escape again Robert? That looks suspiciously like the device they used to ventilate their tunnels!😅

author: @DreadX10
text: Used to have an Lego Excavator with the same set-up to operate the three pneumatic pistons it had. One extra piston had a return-spring and was used as an air-pump. This was around 1984 or so.

author: @normandothegreat
text: Great stuff as always! 😊👍

author: @WayneCarolan
text: A spring return on one side of the bellow and used in a high traffic area would be for sure be interesting.

author: @JohnSmith-qm5xu
text: looks good, clever design adjustments. Works like a bellows in reverse. Newer take on older principles/design. Good stuff.

author: @nic6754
text:
Always love your vids guys. Only subscribed at I think around vid 1800. But like how you weirdly fall in love with the characters from your favourite TV shows, I now laugh a little every time you do Rob. Love it how you laugh when you see your things working ❤
Keep em coming guys. 
Thank you for the awesome content 😊

author: @royksk
text: Looking at the beginning where the bellows are on the bench between your hands gives me an idea. You could dismantle a mouth organ and fit bits to the ends of the bellows to make a musical instrument. As you stretch the bellows air is taken in and then compressing the bellows would force air through the musical bits to make sounds. Needs a bit of work and a name then off to market in my new, Ford Concertina 🤪

author: @sandeepnaik6818
text: How did you cut acrylic?

author: @bethwilton8075
text: Wonderful!❤

author: @steve64464
text: Looks like them really old life support machines they used to have in hospitals 🙂

author: @mrpants8976
text: that way you made it auto change directions made me think about the waggle generator that you made that looked like cattails that swayed in the wind

author: @thetruenolan6655
text: The 24V electric water valves used for sprinkler systems would also work for pressurized air or vacuum. Reed switches and magnets could trigger them, and by moving the magnets be easily adjustable for timing.

author: @dapain1576
text: A possible way to make a water pump? The water flow can cause a syphon and get the bellows moving to push more pressure into a check valve maybe. Unsure really, but a Stirling run on bellows would be a great idea instead of a fan because you could stoke a flame and cool the cold side in one go.

author: @kadmow
text: an air scaler / chisel/ hammer /  etc in some iterations uses a similar valvular regulator - internally...

author: @BeefNEggs057

text: Referring the tiny printed bellows you posted in another video released on T'n'T, this could be used as a motor to drive a little lift pump to move water up a hill at a level of pressure greater than the pressure of the falling water [but slowly, of course].

author: @archibaldvonkranski8881

author: @travismoore7849
text: I Made a  air engine from a electric clock motor and straw, The part of the motor was two steel wheels with some flat pieces on the sides, It was in a housing and worked like a small air turbine when I blowed on it. It was a fun little toy but I never did anything with it.

author: @tenlittleindians
text:
Have you seen the movie "Sam Whiskey" where the blacksmith builds an air pump for underwater diving? Your motor looks a lot like his hand operated air pump.

It's a great western movie with an all Star cast.

replies.text: I was thinking along the same lines. It reminded me of drawings I’ve seen of the air pumps used in the tunnels of Stalag Luft 3 for the ‘Great Escape’. (The actual event, not the movie version)

author: @grendel1960a
text: stunning and spectacular, a less spectacular valve is the simple flap valve, a flap of leather across the air intake (or outside the air exhaust) will allow the one way passage of air quite conveniently, once you have a valve you done even need bellows, 2 airtight bags will do (viking age forge bellows were essentially 2 animal stomachs or 2 leather bags and alternately pushed and pulled (a handle was attached to each for the pushing and pulling)

replies.author: @ogi22
replies.text: Yes, flap valve setup is as old as bellows and it is very, very simple/reliable/time tested. But it's Robert. The tinkerer. He did it for fun and science! 🥰
